stories.title: Kidder Mathews Arranges Sale of Capitol Hill Mixed-Use Apartment Building
stories.excerpt: Casa del Rey, a 30-unit mixed-use apartment building, located at 321 Broadway E. in Seattle’s Capitol Hill neighborhood, has sold for $4.57 million. Matt Johnston, Jerrid Anderson, Matt Laird, and Jack Shephard of Kidder Mathews’ Simon | Anderson Multifamily Team represented the seller, 321 Broadway Associates, LLC, a Seattle-based real estate investment company. The buyer was Sound Asset Partners. Built in 1910, Casa del Rey is among the oldest apartment buildings on Capitol Hill. In addition to its 27 residential units, the property has three ground-floor retail spaces occupied by Buck Horn Barbershop, Spin Cycle Movies, Records, and Games, and a coffee stand. Sound Asset Partners. has experience owning and operating similar assets and recognized the property’s strong in-place cash flow, prime location, excellent light-rail connectivity, and value-add potential through renovations. “The property has a rich history, dating back to the early development of Capitol Hill as one of Seattle’s premier neighborhoods,” said Johnston. The post Kidder Mathews Arranges Sale of Capitol Hill Mixed-Use Apartment Building appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Casa del Rey: 30-unit 1910 mixed-use building at Capitol Hill, Seattle sold for $4.57M
Sound Asset Partners acquired property for in-place cash flow and light-rail transit proximity
Capitol Hill supply remains constrained; older repositionable assets attract institutional buyers
stories.summary: Sound Asset Partners acquired Casa del Rey, a 30-unit historic mixed-use apartment building in Seattle's Capitol Hill for $4.57 million. The property includes 27 residential units and three ground-floor retail spaces. The buyer was drawn to the property's strong cash flow, light-rail connectivity, and value-add renovation potential.
stories.impact: Capital City Market (Seattle Capitol Hill): Institutional acquisition of aging, walkable-transit-adjacent stock signals confidence in neighborhood supply tightness and willingness to value stabilized vintage assets. Suggests limited new supply pipeline and investor appetite for renovation-stage properties in high-demand urban cores, which can compress development feasibility window for new construction competing on location.

stories.title: Scale Supplies $45M Construction Loan for Harlem Condos
stories.excerpt: Mass Development has landed $45 million of construction financing to build a condominium building in Harlem, Commercial Observer has learned. SCALE Lending, a subsidiary of Slate Property Group, provided the 30-month, floating-rate loan with two six-month extension options for the developer’s 11-story condo complex at 264-272 West 135th Street. Mass Development acquired the site — which previously housed two low-rise retail buildings — for $9.3 million last year from Rabina, Crain’s New York Business reported at the time. “Harlem is one of the most supply-constrained condo markets in New York City, with no new project of comparable scale or quality delivered in years, and the pipeline remaining effectively empty,” Martin Nussbaum, co-founder and principal of Slate Property Group, said in a statement. “That level of scarcity creates a rare and compelling opportunity.” Arrow Real Estate Advisors negotiated the debt with a team led by Morris Betesh and Omar Ferreira. The 264-272 West 135th project, which is slated for completion in summer 2028, will feature 72 condos ranging from studios to three-bedroom units. The top floor of the building will feature amenities such as a fitness center, a lounge, a spa, a kids playroom and a movie room. The first two floors of the property will consist of the lobby, 12,000 square feet of retail and a 15,000-square-foot community space, which has already been leased to an undisclosed day care operator. The site is just off Frederick Douglass Boulevard, about a block and a half east of St. Nicholas Park. Mass Development did not immediately return a request for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Mass Development secures $45M construction debt from SCALE Lending for 11-story Harlem condo at 264-272 W 135th St
Harlem condo market supply-constrained; no comparable new project delivered in years, pipeline empty
Developer acquired land for $9.3M; lender sees rare scarcity-driven pricing opportunity
stories.summary: Mass Development closed a $45 million construction loan from SCALE Lending (Slate Property Group subsidiary) for an 11-story condo development at 264-272 West 135th Street in Harlem. The developer acquired the land for $9.3 million and will build on the site of two former low-rise retail buildings. Lender commentary emphasized Harlem's severe condo supply scarcity and empty development pipeline as a rare market opportunity.
stories.impact: Upper Manhattan / Harlem: Exceptional supply vacuum and lender conviction on Harlem's condo market signals strong forward demand and pricing power for luxury multifamily, but also suggests extremely limited competitive threat from new supply for next 2–3 years. For competing developers, the window to acquire Harlem-adjacent sites or pivot to adjacent neighborhoods (East Harlem, Washington Heights) narrows as institutional capital recognizes the scarcity play.

stories.title: Milhaus Merges with SRG Residential, Will Acquire Broadshore Capital
stories.excerpt: Indianapolis-based Milhaus has completed a merger with one Southern California-based multifamily company and has agreed to acquire another. The merger of Milhaus with SRG Residential forms a national, vertically integrated platform focused on developing, owning, and managing apartment communities in more than 20 top U.S. apartment markets. In addition, Milhaus has agreed to acquire Los Angeles-based Broadshore Capital Partners to expand investment and lending capabilities. Chris Payne, former CEO of Newport Beach-based SRG Residential, joins Milhaus as both chief development officer and a shareholder. Jeff Bailey, president of property management at SRG Residential and a shareholder, will continue to lead the combined property management group. “This partnership is a natural fit,” said Tadd Miller, CEO of Milhaus. “SRG Residential brings a best-in-class, high-touch approach to property management and operations along with a quality development pipeline and seasoned leadership, while Milhaus contributes a high-quality owned portfolio, and disciplined development and capital markets infrastructure. Together, we’re creating a platform that is greater than the sum of its parts and exceptionally well positioned for future growth.” The merger with SRG Residential, a subsidiary of Sares Regis Group, adds more than 190 properties and 46,000 units to Milhaus’ third-party property management portfolio. The combined company plans to start eight development projects in 2026 totaling more than 2,000 new units. Broadshore CEO Brad Howe will become CIO at Milhaus when the acquisition closes. Pictured: SRG Residential’s The Monterey in Corona. The post Milhaus Merges with SRG Residential, Will Acquire Broadshore Capital appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Milhaus merges with SRG Residential (subsidiary of Sares Regis); adds 190+ properties, 46K units to PM portfolio
Combined platform plans 8 development starts in 2026 totaling 2K+ units; acquires Broadshore Capital for capital markets/lending
Creates vertically integrated operator across 20+ top markets with development, PM, and debt origination capabilities
stories.summary: Milhaus completed a merger with SRG Residential and agreed to acquire Los Angeles-based Broadshore Capital Partners. The merger adds 190+ properties and 46,000 units under management and brings SRG's CDO (Chris Payne) and PM head (Jeff Bailey) into leadership roles. The combined platform plans to start eight developments in 2026 totaling over 2,000 units and now has in-house lending/capital markets through Broadshore.
stories.impact: National Multifamily: This scale-up consolidation signals institutional capital's appetite for vertically integrated platforms that control development, operations, and debt origination—reducing cost of capital and accelerating project velocity. The 8-project, 2K+ unit pipeline in 2026 demonstrates confidence in rent growth trajectories across 20+ markets and suggests Milhaus will compete harder on capital deployment, potentially raising development land and construction cost expectations for smaller, non-integrated competitors in core markets.

stories.title: Department of Defense Inks 24K-SF Deal at Former Long Island HQ of 1-800-Flowers
stories.excerpt: The U.S. Department of Defense is opening a new regional office in the Long Island hamlet of Carle Place, N.Y. The department, which is seeking approval this year from Congress to rebrand as the Department of War, signed its new lease at 1 Old Country Road in Nassau County. The five-story office building recently served as the headquarters of 1-800-Flowers. The online floral and gift retailer departed the repositioned office building for Jericho, N.Y., in 2020. The Defense Department’s 23,600-square-foot lease was signed for a 10-year term with landlord DBD Realty Group. The latter was represented in the negotiations by Kelly Koukou of Lee & Associates NYC, while CBRE’s Ralph Guiffre, Vince Lamatta and Richard Freel represented the tenant. The 320,000-square-foot office building recently underwent a $22 million repositioning, including a new facade, windows, lobby and upgraded infrastructure. Its newly constructed parking deck also offers capacity for 1,600 vehicles. “The Department of War’s commitment further validates the extensive repositioning of the building and reflects the continued momentum we’re seeing as the property fills following its post-pandemic leasing surge,” Koukou said in a statement. The department is expected to take occupancy early next year following an extensive buildout of its future space. Another government tenant, the Federal Aviation Administration, occupies just over 74,000 square feet across the fifth and fourth stories of the building, where it arrived for a 15-year term in 2023. Spokespeople for the department, DBD and CBRE did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: DoD leases 23.6K SF at former 1-800-Flowers HQ in Carle Place, Long Island for 10-year term
Property underwent $22M repositioning; 320K SF office building fully redeveloped with new parking (1.6K spaces)
Government anchor tenant validates suburban Nassau County office market and repositioning economics
stories.summary: The U.S. Department of Defense signed a 23,600-square-foot, 10-year lease at One Old Country Road in Carle Place, Long Island (Nassau County), the former 1-800-Flowers headquarters. The property underwent a $22 million repositioning including facade, windows, lobby, and new 1,600-space parking deck. CBRE and Lee & Associates represented parties.
stories.impact: Long Island / Nassau County Suburban Office: Federal government anchor tenant validates repositioning thesis for aging suburban office stock and signals willingness to pay market rent for modernized facilities. This reduces risk perception for multifamily developers considering acquisition of adjacent suburban-office-to-residential conversion opportunities in established commuter corridors, though primary relevance is to office/mixed-use, not pure multifamily supply/demand.

stories.title: Habitat for Humanity SKKC Completes Largest Affordable Housing Building to Date
stories.excerpt: Habitat for Humanity Seattle-King & Kittitas Counties (Habitat SKKC) will celebrate the completion of the 58-home Liberty Commons community in Seattle’s Columbia City neighborhood. Liberty Commons is Habitat SKKC’s largest single-building development to date—and the largest multi-family building in Habitat for Humanity’s history. Located at 5022 Martin Luther King Jr. Way S., Liberty Commons features five stories of condominiums, including five studios, 26 one-bedroom units, and 27 two-bedroom units with homes ranging in size from 370 to 690 square feet. It also includes a 2,000-square-foot outdoor courtyard. Located in Seattle’s culturally and economically diverse Columbia City neighborhood, Liberty Commons is a transit-oriented development. The project was delivered in partnership with Exxel Pacific. Liberty Commons was made possible through the support of Washington State, the City of Seattle, Washington State Department of Commerce, Amazon, the National Housing Trust, Washington Trust Bank, and the Washington State Department of Ecology. The post Habitat for Humanity SKKC Completes Largest Affordable Housing Building to Date appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Habitat for Humanity SKKC completes Liberty Commons: 58-unit affordable condo in Seattle's Columbia City, largest Habitat multifamily ever
Mix: 5 studios, 26 one-bed, 27 two-bed units (370–690 SF); transit-oriented; delivered in partnership with Exxel Pacific
Funded by Washington State, City of Seattle, Amazon, and National Housing Trust; demonstrates nonprofit-private development model
stories.summary: Habitat for Humanity Seattle-King & Kittitas Counties completed Liberty Commons, a 58-unit affordable condo building in Columbia City (Seattle) with units ranging from 370 to 690 square feet. The five-story development is the largest multifamily project in Habitat for Humanity's history. It was delivered in partnership with private developer Exxel Pacific with support from Washington State, Seattle, Amazon, and the National Housing Trust.
stories.impact: Seattle / Columbia City: Large-scale nonprofit affordable housing delivery in transit-oriented urban neighborhood reduces demand pressure on market-rate rental stock and anchors lower-income cohort in appreciating neighborhood. For market-rate developers, this signals: (1) public/philanthropic capital focusing on supply gaps below market rate; (2) Columbia City becoming institutional affordable-housing target, raising land costs and reducing developable land for for-profit multifamily; (3) partnership models with nonprofits may open tax-credit/grant funding pathways but reduce velocity and pricing power.

stories.title: Alterra IOS Secures $400M Refinancing From Truist, KeyBank
stories.excerpt: Alterra IOS announced the closing of a $400 million refinancing led by Truist Financial Corp. and KeyBank, supporting the continued expansion of its growing industrial outdoor storage platform. Secured by a portfolio of 99 IOS properties spanning 27 states, the financing was executed utilizing an equity pledge framework in place of traditional asset-level mortgages. The structure enables streamlined execution and portfolio-level underwriting. Truist provided $225 million, and KeyBank National Association committed $175 million. The transaction follows several significant funding transactions for Alterra, including a $244 million equity-based pledge issued by Blackstone Real Estate Debt Strategies. “Structures like this are becoming more relevant as institutional capital seeks efficient ways to access fragmented sectors at scale,” said Kate Mooney, Alterra Senior Associate, Capital Markets. “As IOS portfolios have grown and matured, lenders have developed greater comfort underwriting diversified portfolios rather than individual assets.” Connect Industrial West | August 20 | Irvine, CAOn August 20, Connect Industrial West will bring together the owners, investors, developers, brokers, lenders, and occupiers driving industrial real estate across the Western U.S. Join top decision-makers for an afternoon of market insights, dealmaking opportunities, and networking with the industry’s most influential players. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post Alterra IOS Secures $400M Refinancing From Truist, KeyBank appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Alterra IOS closes $400M refinancing: $225M Truist + $175M KeyBank for portfolio of 99 industrial outdoor storage properties across 27 states
Financing uses equity-pledge framework (not asset-level mortgages); portfolio-level underwriting signals lender comfort with fragmented asset class at scale
Structure innovation reflects maturing IOS market and institutional capital appetite for non-traditional real estate
stories.summary: Alterra IOS closed a $400 million portfolio-level refinancing led by Truist ($225M) and KeyBank ($175M) secured by 99 industrial outdoor storage properties across 27 states. The financing used an equity pledge structure instead of traditional asset-level mortgages, enabling streamlined underwriting. The transaction follows a $244 million equity-based pledge from Blackstone Real Estate Debt Strategies.
stories.impact: Non-multifamily asset class (industrial outdoor storage): Largely irrelevant to multifamily development strategy, but structure innovation (portfolio-level equity pledges vs. asset mortgages) signals lender appetite for covenant-light, diversified collateral baskets that may become model for multifamily portfolios. Institutional capital's comfort with fragmented IOS may reduce appetite for single-asset or small-portfolio multifamily debt, favoring larger platform players.

stories.title: JV Acquires 174K-SF Denver Office Portfolio
stories.excerpt: Knightbridge and Argosy acquired a two-building office portfolio for $14 million. The buildings, Three Maroon, built in 2001, and Maroon Five, built in 2008, combined, make up 174,000 square feet. The 4-story buildings sit on 10 acres in the Meridian International Business Center. Mark IV Capital was the seller. The Colorado Real Estate Journal reports that a Knightbridge spokesman said the acquisition closed at a meaningful discount to replacement cost and prior sales prices of both assets. The blended occupancy of the two buildings averaged out at 88% with the average lease at 4.4 years. Cushman & Wakefield’s Aaron Johnson and Jon Hendrickson represented Mark IV. 1st Interstate Bank provided acquisition financing. Since 2024, Knightbridge has acquired three area office buildings. The post JV Acquires 174K-SF Denver Office Portfolio appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Knightbridge and Argosy JV acquire 174K SF Denver office portfolio (Two Maroon, Maroon Five) for $14M from Mark IV Capital
Purchase at meaningful discount to replacement cost; 88% blended occupancy, avg lease term 4.4 years; Knightbridge acquired 3 Denver office buildings since 2024
Trend signals opportunistic office acquisition in secondary markets; conversion potential unclear
stories.summary: Knightbridge and Argosy acquired a two-building, 174,000-square-foot Denver office portfolio (Three Maroon, Maroon Five) in the Meridian International Business Center for $14 million. The purchase closed at a discount to replacement cost, with blended occupancy at 88% and average lease term of 4.4 years. This represents Knightbridge's third Denver office acquisition since 2024.
stories.impact: Denver Office/Flex: Repeat opportunistic office acquisitions at discount valuations suggest either speculative hold-for-conversion or assignment for sale to conversion-focused developers (e.g., office-to-multifamily). For multifamily developers, this signals: (1) distressed office assets available at low basis in secondary markets; (2) investor interest in near-term office repricing; (3) conversion economics may improve if cap rates widen further. Monitor if Knightbridge or Argosy begin office-to-residential conversions—signals growing feasibility in Denver.

stories.title: Affinius Capital Provides $35M Refi for Grocery-Office Asset Near Houston
stories.excerpt: Howard Hughes Communities has secured a $35 million loan to refinance Village Green at Bridgeland Central, a nearly 200,000-square-foot mixed-use development that includes retail and office space in Cypress, Texas, Commercial Observer can first report. Affinius Capital provided the debt, while JLL’s Colby Mueck, Kelly Layne, Jack Britton and Scot Sarlin arranged the transaction. Located at 20115 Bridgeland Creek Parkway in Cypress — an unincorporated community in Harris County, a town roughly 25 miles northwest of Downtown Houston — Village Green at Bridgeland Central is a 23-acre development that opened in 2024. The asset is nearly fully leased and includes a 149,000-square-foot retail center with Texas grocery store H-E-B as an anchor tenant, and a 49,000-square-foot Class A office building. Costa Kontoulis, managing director at Affinius Capital, noted in a statement that Village Green at Bridgeland Central has attracted “high-quality tenants” in both its office and retail components. “This financing extends our relationship with Howard Hughes and reflects our conviction in well-located, grocery-anchored retail paired with sustainability-focused office space,” he said. Brian Pascus can be reached at bpascus@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Howard Hughes Communities refinances Village Green at Bridgeland Central (Cypress, TX) for $35M via Affinius Capital
Nearly 200K SF mixed-use (149K SF retail with H-E-B anchor, 49K SF Class A office); opened 2024, nearly fully leased
Cypress (Harris County, 25 mi NW Houston): Institutional debt appetite for stabilized grocery-anchored retail with office component
stories.summary: Howard Hughes Communities secured a $35 million refinance from Affinius Capital for Village Green at Bridgeland Central, a nearly 200,000-square-foot mixed-use development in Cypress, Texas that opened in 2024. The asset includes a 149,000-square-foot retail center anchored by H-E-B and a 49,000-square-foot Class A office building, and is nearly fully leased.
stories.impact: Houston Metro / Cypress Suburban: Rapid stabilization and refinance of mixed-use asset signals strong-performing suburban Houston development and investor confidence in grocery-anchored retail durability. For multifamily: Affirms Cypress/Bridgeland as institutional-quality submarket with long-term institutional landlord (Howard Hughes) backing dense, mixed-use development. Developers should monitor Bridgeland's continued absorption and rent growth; suburban Houston multifamily likely to benefit from demand spillover from constrained central Houston.

stories.title: Union Leaders Cite Inspection Process in Pfizer Conversion’s Near-Collapse
stories.excerpt: The jury may be out on the direct cause of the near-collapse of the former Pfizer headquarters in Midtown last week, but union officials say the city can prevent such disasters with better inspections. The swaying, sagging and shattering of 235 East 42nd Street put the commercial real estate industry, the local government and anyone within a five-block radius on high alert on July 7. Meanwhile, Nathan Berman, the main developer of the office-to-residential conversion at the former corporate headquarters, downplayed in the media what could have been a catastrophic disaster, further enraging the organizations who advocate for public and worker safety. For now, there are no definitive answers as to what really happened — but organized labor has a pretty good idea of where to start finding them. Union leaders shocked and angered by the near-disaster spent the last week railing against the city’s Professional Certification program and third-party Special Inspection Agency (SIA) approval process. Developers typically hire SIAs rather than have New York City Department of Buildings (DOB) officials approve work themselves. In the case of 235 East 42nd Street, Berman’s Metro Loft and David Werner Real Estate Investments hired Domani Inspection Services to certify the work. Domani had decided work was done properly before the steel beams on the 21st floor began to buckle, leaving floors sagging up to four inches and windows shattered. Domani has been associated with numerous violations in other projects and was widely cited in documents pertaining to the Pfizer building’s office-to-residential conversion project, according to The New York Times. “The developer has publicly characterized this breach of integrity in the structural steel as a construction mishap — this is not a mishap, this is much more serious,” Gary LaBarbera, president of the Building and Construction Trades Council of Greater New York, told Commercial Observer. “On this project, they hired a company that was
bullets: Pfizer HQ office-to-residential conversion (235 E 42nd St, Midtown Manhattan) suffered partial near-collapse on July 7; structural cause unclear
Developer Nathan Berman (MetroLoft) downplayed severity; union/labor leaders blame inadequate city inspection and third-party SIA approval processes
Incident raises regulatory/liability risk for office-to-residential conversions in NYC; heightens scrutiny of developer oversight
stories.summary: The former Pfizer headquarters building at 235 East 42nd Street in Midtown Manhattan, undergoing office-to-residential conversion by MetroLoft's Nathan Berman, experienced a partial structural failure on July 7. While the cause remains unclear, union leaders blamed the city's Professional Certification program and third-party Special Inspection Agency (SIA) oversight process, alleging inadequate inspections. Berman downplayed the incident in media commentary.
stories.impact: NYC Midtown Office-to-Residential Conversions: High-profile structural incident in major conversion project will likely trigger enhanced DOB inspection protocols, slower permitting, and higher insurance/liability costs for future office-to-residential conversions in Manhattan. This increases development timeline and contingency reserves, potentially squeezing conversion project economics—especially for older office stock in historic districts requiring structural remediation. Developers should assume tighter inspection regimes and increased design/engineering costs for all large-scale NYC conversions post-incident.

stories.title: Barolo Restaurant Group Inks Deal to Open Italian Eatery in Times Square
stories.excerpt: Italian restaurant operator Barolo Restaurant Group plans to open its third New York City restaurant, Barolo West, at 250 West 49th Street in Times Square. The company took 2,500 square feet of space at the newly developed 29-story property in Midtown. The length of the lease was not disclosed, but the asking rent was $210 per square foot, according to broker Meridian Capital Group. Meridian’s Noam Aziz and Carson Shahrabani represented Barolo and landlord Chess Builders. “Times Square continues to attract best-in-class restaurant operators, and this space checked every box, with prominent frontage, a flexible layout, and a brand-new building at the center of the Theater District,” Aziz said in a statement. “It was a pleasure working with ownership and the Barolo team to bring this lease across the finish line.” Barolo Restaurant Group also owns Barbaresco at 843 Lexington Avenue on the Upper East Side and Barolo East at 214 East 49th Street in Midtown East. “Barolo West is a natural next step for a group with a devoted following on the East Side,” Shahrabani said in a statement. “Representing both sides of the transaction allowed our team to move efficiently and structure a lease that worked well for the landlord and the tenant alike. We are confident the restaurant will be a wonderful addition to the West 49th Street corridor.” Also known as Starline Tower, 250 West 49th Street is a mixed-use building with 138 luxury apartment units. Tenant amenities include a rooftop terrace, a billiard lounge and coworking spaces. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Barolo Restaurant Group signs 2.5K SF ground-floor lease at 250 West 49th St (Times Square, newly developed 29-story building)
Asking rent $210/SF; Times Square retail market attracts flagship restaurant operators despite high rents and footfall uncertainty
Small lease signal; weak multifamily relevance except ground-floor activation in new residential buildings
stories.summary: Barolo Restaurant Group signed a 2,500-square-foot lease at 250 West 49th Street in Times Square for its third New York City location, Barolo West. The asking rent was $210 per square foot in the newly developed 29-story building. Barolo also operates restaurants on the Upper East Side and Midtown East.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Small retail lease in a new residential building reflects ground-floor activation strategy common in urban multifamily; high rent ($210/SF) reflects Times Square scarcity and tourist footfall, but does not signal demand patterns affecting apartment rents or feasibility in other markets.

stories.title: Housing Notes: Developers are in on the joke: Apologize later
stories.excerpt: I have long had a fascination with the grit it takes to be a Manhattan developer. After all, I taught market analysis to a class of approximately 150 graduate students in Columbia’s Master of Science in Real Estate Development (MSRED) program for 8 years. My students have ended up all over the U.S. and hopefully they learned not to accept “no” as an answer when they’re out in the world. That’s the dividing line since developers tend to ignore the word “no” and keep pushing, when non-developers like me would cower in fear and self-doubt. It helps to look at […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Opinion piece reflects on Manhattan developer psychology and business practices; contrasts Harry Macklowe's willingness to push regulatory boundaries with Nathan Berman's lower profile
Author notes cyclical developer model: 'Developers develop until they can't develop anymore. It usually ends badly, but 5 years later they start over.'
References Pfizer conversion structural incident and developer reputation in context of larger industry behavior patterns
stories.summary: A real estate market analyst reflects on the mindset and behavior patterns of Manhattan developers over 40 years, noting their tendency to ignore regulatory obstacles and push forward aggressively. The piece contrasts legendary developer Harry Macklowe with the quieter Nathan Berman (of the Pfizer conversion), highlighting that developers typically cycle through boom-bust patterns and resume operations quickly. The Pfizer structural incident is cited as example of downside risk.
stories.impact: Broadly relevant as cultural/market commentary but not actionable for specific development projects. Suggests that high-profile structural failures and regulatory crackdowns may have limited lasting impact on developer behavior; however, project-specific risk (insurance, liability, permitting delays) will likely increase for office-to-residential conversions in NYC in near term.

stories.title: Buyer scores $2M judgment against Mandarin Oriental Boca developer
stories.excerpt: The legal troubles at Penn-Florida’s Mandarin Oriental Boca Raton continue to mount after the developer lost a $2 million judgment.  A Palm Beach County Circuit Court judge sided with Douglas and Debra Jacobs after they filed a lawsuit against Penn-Florida affiliate Via Mizner Owner III, which alleged the developer failed to complete their condo in time, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  The developer announced plans for the 248-unit hotel-condo Mandarin Oriental in 2015, with an initial opening target of 2017, the outlet reported. The complaint alleged that the Jacobses signed a contract to buy a condo in the development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Penn-Florida affiliate Via Mizner Owner III loses $2M judgment to condo buyers Douglas & Debra Jacobs at Mandarin Oriental Boca Raton
Buyers contracted 2022 for Dec 31, 2025 delivery; project delayed since 2015 announcement with 2017 initial target; 13 buyer lawsuits filed total
Judge ruled buyers can demand payment from title companies; developer signals motion for rehearing on conflicting ruling grounds
stories.summary: A Palm Beach County judge ruled against Penn-Florida affiliate Via Mizner Owner III in a lawsuit by condo buyers Douglas and Debra Jacobs, awarding a $2 million judgment for failure to deliver their unit by the December 31, 2025 contractual deadline. The Mandarin Oriental Boca Raton hotel-condo was first announced in 2015 with a 2017 opening target. Thirteen buyers have filed lawsuits for breach of contract; one settlement of $2.1 million was also reached. The developer plans a motion for rehearing.
stories.impact: South Florida Luxury Condo Market (Boca Raton): Prolonged delivery delays and buyer litigation at high-end condo project signals continued stress in luxury market despite limited supply. For developers: (1) damages awards ($2M+) and escrow claims reduce project cash flow and refinance/exit flexibility; (2) repeated delays erode buyer confidence and increase presale cancellation risk; (3) similar delays across Mandarin Oriental projects (NYC, Boca) suggest broader developer/contractor challenges (labor, materials, financing). Multifamily developers should monitor if luxury rental alternative captures displaced Mandarin condo buyers seeking near-term occupancy.

stories.title: Chicago proposes $287M TIF raid for Related Midwest’s megaproject The 78
stories.excerpt: Chicago officials are set to throw $287 million of property tax revenue into the megaproject that includes Major League Soccer team the Chicago Fire’s new stadium in the South Loop.  The proposal for the project known as the 78 is being considered by the Chicago City Council Finance Committee. The shift in funding is expected to partially reorganize a tax increment financing subsidy for the project’s developer, Related Midwest, according to Crain’s.  The revised agreement would add $287 million from the Canal/Congress TIF district to the TIF district that the 78 sits in, meaning the project’s developers would not have […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago proposes $287M TIF funding transfer to Related Midwest's The 78 megaproject (South Loop, includes Chicago Fire MLS stadium)
Plan redirects Canal/Congress TIF revenue to 78's Roosevelt/Clark TIF zone; original agreement was $85M over 3 years; new proposal could exhaust Canal/Congress TIF for 9 years
Alderman Bill Conway opposes raid on Union Station maintenance funds; city views 78 as economic catalyst for area
stories.summary: Chicago officials proposed redirecting $287 million in property tax revenue from the Canal/Congress Tax Increment Financing district to fund infrastructure around Related Midwest's The 78 megaproject in the South Loop. The 78 is a mixed-use urban renewal project that includes the Chicago Fire's new MLS stadium. The original TIF agreement allocated $85 million over three years; the new proposal could exhaust Canal/Congress TIF revenue for nine years and risks reducing funds for Union Station maintenance. Alderman Bill Conway raised concerns about the raid.
stories.impact: Chicago South Loop: Major public subsidy commitment ($287M TIF redirect) de-risks The 78's infrastructure and reduces Related Midwest's development costs, but creates fiscal crowding-out for other neighborhood improvements. For multifamily: (1) Public capital concentration on The 78 and Fire stadium may elevate land values in South Loop, pressuring smaller development sites; (2) stadium-anchored mixed-use development will drive incremental foot traffic and demand in South Loop residential, benefiting both The 78's units and nearby market-rate apartments; (3) long-term TIF exhaustion may limit public incentives for competing residential projects post-2034. Developers should acquire South Loop sites early before land premiums fully capitalize The 78's demand uplift.

stories.title: Shvo gets fraud claims tossed in Mandarin Oriental suit
stories.excerpt: Developer Michael Shvo is almost off the hook at the Mandarin Oriental.  A judge in New York Supreme Court last week dismissed claims brought against Shvo and his companies by two condo owners at his Mandarin Oriental Residences, who had alleged fraud and shoddy construction.  The judge’s dismissal likely takes some pressure off Shvo, who has been dealing with numerous lawsuits and disputes in the past year.  “We are pleased that the Court dismissed the overwhelming majority of plaintiffs’ claims, including all claims against Michael Shvo personally,” Shvo attorney Morris Missry said in a statement. “The decision confirms our long-held […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Judge dismisses fraud and construction defect claims against Michael Shvo and companies in Mandarin Oriental Residences condo suit (685 Fifth Ave, NYC)
Buyers John & Diane Goodman alleged sponsor failed to disclose prior litigation history (Bilgili allegations of misuse of funds, 2020 settlement); also alleged substandard construction
Shvo/Bilgili partnership dissolved 2020 post-settlement; dismissal removes pressure on Shvo but underlying condo quality disputes remain
stories.summary: A New York Supreme Court judge dismissed the majority of claims, including fraud charges, brought against developer Michael Shvo by condo owners John and Diane Goodman at Mandarin Oriental Residences at 685 Fifth Avenue. The owners alleged Shvo failed to disclose litigation history with Turkish investor Serdar Bilgili (who alleged misuse of project funds in 2020 and later settled) and alleged construction defects in their $6.1 million one-bedroom unit. The judge's dismissal significantly reduces Shvo's litigation exposure.
stories.impact: NYC Luxury Condo Market (Upper East Side): Judicial dismissal reduces Shvo's liability but does not eliminate underlying condo quality perception issues in the market. For multifamily: (1) Luxury condo market stress (delays, litigation, quality disputes) may drive higher-income renters toward market-rate apartments with shorter lease commitments and no construction risk; (2) Mandarin Oriental condo presales weakness (if any) could signal demand shift to rental over ownership in NYC luxury segment; (3) Shvo's operational challenges may pressure future presale velocity, indirectly strengthening rental market demand in similar Upper East Side/Fifth Avenue corridor.

stories.title: Judge tosses suit over Two Roads’ Four Seasons Bahamas. Not so fast, plaintiff says
stories.excerpt: A judge tossed a lawsuit accusing Two Roads Development of sidelining a partner from a roughly $300 million Four Seasons-branded condo project in the Bahamas, but the fight isn’t over.  The partner that sued, Soho Development, led by Delray Beach resident and attorney Roger Stein, says the judge addressed the wrong complaint and not the latest filing. Soho Development last summer sued Two Roads, a South Florida luxury development firm, alleging it excluded Soho from the development, while continuing to use project contracts and confidential information provided by Soho with no compensation to the firm, according to the complaint filed […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Judge dismisses lawsuit by Soho Development (founder Roger Stein) against Two Roads Development over $300M Four Seasons Bahamas condo project (Paradise Island)
Soho alleged Two Roads excluded it from development while using Soho's contracts/confidential info; Two Roads called suit 'unabashed money grab'; project 67 units, presales underway, completion 2028
Soho claims judge addressed wrong complaint filing; dispute over non-compete and circumvention provisions continues
stories.summary: A judge dismissed a lawsuit filed by Soho Development against Two Roads Development regarding a roughly $300 million Four Seasons-branded condo project (Ocean Club, Four Seasons Residences) on Paradise Island, Bahamas. Soho claimed Two Roads excluded it from development while using Soho's proprietary information and contracts. Two Roads denied wrongdoing and called the suit an extortion attempt. The 67-unit project (two to five-bedroom units) is in presales with completion expected in 2028. Soho signals it will refile.
stories.impact: Bahamas Luxury Condo Market: Partnership disputes and litigation over luxury condo projects signal heightened deal complexity and risk in high-net-worth development. However, project remains on track (presales underway, 2028 completion), suggesting demand for ultra-luxury Bahamas beachfront residences remains intact despite partner friction. Weak relevance to multifamily strategy except as indicator of offshore capital deployment trends and legal/partnership risk in mixed-use/resort developments.

stories.title: Blake Poston jumps from the gridiron to the high-rise with plans for a $100M Uptown Dallas apartment project
stories.excerpt: A former SMU football player is developing a $100 million, 20-story high-rise just north of Uptown Dallas, around a mile from where he used to drop into coverage on Saturdays.  Blake Poston, who was a part of the Southern Methodist University Mustangs football team for three years and went to Highland Park High School, is also the founder of Cover2 Capital. Poston told the Dallas Morning News that Cover2 is aiming to begin construction on an Uptown high-rise at 5220 McKinney Avenue late next year. The building is dubbed The Savannah, and Cover2 is still in design and city development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Blake Poston (former SMU football player, founder Cover2 Capital) developing $100M 20-story apartment high-rise in Uptown Dallas at 5220 McKinney Ave

The Savannah: 100-unit luxury rental with 1–3 bedrooms, avg unit 1,800 SF, 3 penthouses; construction planned late 2025; also developing 297-unit Wheelhouse (N Richland Hills) and Greerhouse (Anna garden-style)
Poston founded Cover2 in 2022 post-S2 Capital; boutique development model targeting premium rents comparable to nearby luxury properties
stories.summary: Blake Poston, founder of Cover2 Capital and former SMU football player, is developing The Savannah, a $100 million, 20-story luxury apartment tower in Uptown Dallas at 5220 McKinney Avenue. The project will include 100 units (mix of one to three-bedroom apartments and three penthouses) with an average unit size of 1,800 square feet. Construction is planned to begin late 2025. Poston's firm is also behind Wheelhouse (297 units in North Richland Hills) and Greerhouse (garden-style development in Anna).
stories.impact: Dallas Uptown Market: Young developer's $100M Uptown high-rise with outsized units (1,800 SF avg) and premium positioning signals confidence in luxury apartment demand and rent growth in established Dallas submarket. For competitive developers: (1) Uptown's ability to support >$100M boutique project indicates strong institutional-quality rental demand and likely tight supply of new luxury units; (2) Cover2's emphasis on larger units and premium finishes targets high-income demographic seeking space over density—possible alternative positioning to traditional luxury; (3) late-2025 start timing suggests current entitlements feasible; pipeline visibility is limited. Monitor Uptown's absorption and rent growth through Savannah's delivery (likely 2027–2028) to gauge demand trajectory.
